patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 32-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping when I walk.” Early in the visit, volunteer: tight/stiff legs worse when walking faster or climbing stairs; frequent tripping/feet catching; unsteady on stairs needing the railing; nighttime calf cramps. If asked about progression, add: worse by end of day; mornings feel “locked up” and slow to start; avoiding long walks due to fear of falling; uneven shoe wear and scuffed shoe tops. If asked about bladder/bowel, initially downplay; only admit urgency and occasional leakage if directly asked (it’s embarrassing). Mention constipation only if asked. If asked about neurologic symptoms, disclose: two seizures in past year with stiffening/shaking ~1 minute and confusion after; occasional jerks when falling asleep; occasional slurred speech when very tired; mild numb/tingly feet. Express misconceptions: worry it’s a peripheral nerve problem like Charcot-Marie-Tooth due to high arches/clumsiness; assume genetic testing won’t be covered and is unaffordable; believe seizure meds are always expensive lifelong. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and become more willing to share the urinary leakage and fears.

medications: Baclofen 10 mg PO three times daily
confounders: Borderline vitamin B12 deficiency (elevated methylmalonic acid) that can mimic/worsen myelopathy and gait disturbance
Hypomagnesemia that can lower seizure threshold and contribute to breakthrough seizures
SSRI use (sertraline) which can contribute to hyponatremia or myoclonus-like symptoms, complicating seizure evaluation
Neurogenic bladder symptoms can be misattributed to multiple sclerosis or spinal cord pathology rather than hereditary spastic paraplegia

required_evidence: family history or pathogenic variant on genetic testing
no peripheral neuropathy pattern on exam/EMG to suggest Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease

medications: diazepam rectal gel 0.2 mg/kg PR once for seizure >5 minutes (max 20 mg)

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of hereditary spastic paraplegia to guide counseling, workup, and supportive management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "hereditary spastic paraplegia" and explains it in plain language (genetic/degenerative upper motor neuron syndrome causing progressive lower-limb spasticity and gait difficulty).
Clarifies uncertainty appropriately (e.g., probable vs confirmed) and indicates that genetic testing/family history can help confirm subtype when relevant.

Elicits key red flags that would suggest alternative diagnoses requiring urgent evaluation (acute onset, severe back pain, bowel/bladder retention, rapidly progressive weakness, sensory level, fever, cancer history).
Performs a brief functional assessment (gait/falls, stairs, activities of daily living) and screens for common associated features (urinary urgency, leg cramps, neuropathic pain, fatigue).
Provides a basic initial management overview centered on supportive care (physiotherapy, stretching, mobility aids, spasticity management options) and safety (fall prevention).
Meets communication milestone: includes the exact term "hereditary spastic paraplegia" during diagnosis explanation.

Addresses treatment communication milestone by mentioning "levetiracetam" in the treatment discussion (e.g., only if seizures/comorbid epilepsy are present; otherwise clarifies it is not a standard treatment for hereditary spastic paraplegia).

Avoids unsafe prescribing: does not initiate any medication without first checking allergies and contraindications when moving toward prescribing decisions (even though prescribing is not required for L1).
Does not claim disease-modifying therapy exists when none is established; sets expectations about gradual progression and symptom-focused management.
Uses shared decision-making language and offers follow-up/return precautions for new neurologic deficits or red-flag symptoms.

minimal_paths.description: Arrive at and document hereditary spastic paraplegia as the primary diagnosis with a brief explanation and basic safety-netting.
must_collect: spasticity symptoms (stiff legs, scissoring gait, cramps)
absence of acute red flags (bowel/bladder retention, sensory level, severe back pain, fever)
steps: Elicit history focusing on progressive lower-limb spasticity and gait impairment, onset/tempo, falls, and functional impact.
Ask about family history and prior neurologic diagnoses; review systems for urinary urgency and other associated symptoms.
Screen for red flags and alternative etiologies (acute/subacute onset, sensory level, severe pain, systemic symptoms, cancer/infection risk).
Explain diagnosis using the term "hereditary spastic paraplegia" and describe typical course and what it means.
Document diagnosis via record_diagnosis.
Discuss general supportive management options and provide return precautions for red flags.

acceptable_variants.description: Probable HSP with plan for confirmation; emphasizes differential and need to exclude mimics while still documenting HSP as working diagnosis.
steps: Gather history emphasizing chronic progressive spastic paraparesis and family history; assess urinary symptoms and falls.
State working diagnosis as hereditary spastic paraplegia while acknowledging confirmation may require genetic testing and exclusion of mimics.
Briefly discuss key differentials to be excluded (e.g., multiple sclerosis, spinal cord compression, B12 deficiency, ALS/PLS) and red-flag symptoms prompting urgent evaluation.
Explain supportive care focus and safety-netting; mention levetiracetam only in context of comorbid seizures (not as routine HSP therapy).
Record diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis-first flow: agent provides explanation early, then confirms key historical elements and documents diagnosis.
steps: State that symptoms are most consistent with hereditary spastic paraplegia and explain the condition in plain language.
Then confirm chronic progression, family history, and absence of acute red flags; assess function and urinary symptoms.
Provide supportive management overview and return precautions; include levetiracetam only if relevant to seizures or explicitly note it is not standard for HSP.
Record diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious but clear-speaking college student. Start with: “I’ve got the worst headache of my life and I feel really sick.” Immediately volunteer: sudden severe headache over a few hours, high fever/chills since last night, stiff neck worse looking down, light sensitivity, vomiting, and feeling confused/can’t think straight. Act worried and slightly slowed; you may doze off if the interview drags.

If asked, add: extreme fatigue/weakness, body aches, dizziness on standing, faster breathing/occasional shortness of breath, sore throat/runny nose a few days ago, cold hands/feet, and one brief episode of word-finding trouble with right arm heaviness that resolved.

Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked/examined: low urine today, non-blanching purple leg spots, blotchy skin, eye pain with movement.

Resist sharing at first (admit only if asked directly): crowded party/sharing drinks, sick roommate, and missing some teen vaccines (embarrassed).

Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s my hemiplegic migraine,” “maybe food poisoning/flu,” “neck pain might be from sleeping wrong.”

If clinician shows empathy, soften and share more. Ask about cheaper options: “Can I take oral antibiotics at home? I can’t afford admission and I can’t miss work.”

Blood culture.value: Gram-negative diplococci seen; Neisseria meningitidis PCR positive

medications: Verapamil ER 120 mg PO nightly (migraine prophylaxis)
Rizatriptan 10 mg PO as needed for migraine (max 30 mg/day)
confounders: Hemiplegic migraine attacks can mimic acute stroke/meningoencephalitis due to transient unilateral weakness and aphasia
Recent use of triptan may cause chest tightness/palpitations and can confound evaluation of tachycardia/anxiety
Early meningococcal disease may present with nonspecific viral-like symptoms before rash/meningismus develops

required_evidence: hemodynamic instability or altered mental status

Before prescribing any medication, explicitly confirms/addresses: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; if information is missing, the agent asks for it and defers prescribing until clarified (critical safety behavior).

Recommends pyridostigmine as a symptomatic option when appropriate for CMS and provides key counseling (dosing schedule concept, expected benefits, common adverse effects such as GI cramping/diarrhea, and when to seek help for worsening weakness).

Explicitly advises to avoid macrolides (e.g., azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin) due to risk of exacerbating neuromuscular weakness; offers safer alternatives or advises clinician review if antibiotics are needed.
Assesses and communicates red flags requiring urgent evaluation (e.g., bulbar/respiratory weakness, aspiration, rapidly progressive weakness) and provides clear escalation instructions.

Avoids contraindicated/low-value interventions for CMS glycosylation defects (e.g., immunosuppression or IVIG as routine treatment for presumed autoimmune MG) unless there is clear separate indication.
Uses clear, patient-centered language and checks understanding; includes a brief plan for monitoring response and adverse effects after starting therapy.

Thinks vaccines can 'overload' his immune system because he already has low B-cells, so his caregiver worries vaccines are unsafe.
Caregiver assumes antibiotics should be started every time a fever happens, even when there are no localizing symptoms.
Believes his anemia is caused mainly by not eating enough iron and that high-dose iron supplements will fix it quickly.
Caregiver thinks transfusions are 'addictive' and that once started he will need them forever, so tries to avoid them even when recommended.
Attributes developmental delays to 'not trying hard enough' and worries that therapy is optional rather than medically necessary.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s parent and speak for most of the visit. You are anxious and give short answers unless asked directly. Start by saying: “He keeps getting fevers out of nowhere every few weeks, with chills, and then he’s wiped out for days.” Volunteer only: sudden recurrent fevers, looks very tired/pale even between episodes, can’t keep up with other kids, needs lots of rest, and is behind in speech/learning.

If the clinician asks, add: sore throat and swollen neck glands during fevers, mouth sores around fevers, frequent long-lasting colds, more ear/sinus infections, poor appetite/slow weight gain, short of breath or dizzy with running, easy bruising/nosebleeds sometimes, and belly discomfort/feels full fast.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: occasional yellow eyes after fevers, dark urine once or twice, headaches/light sensitivity during fevers, cold hands/feet, grayish skin tone, leg/bone aches, or being told his spleen felt “a bit big.”

Express misconceptions: you think it’s “just the flu,” maybe allergies, Lyme, or stomach bugs. Be guarded about missed vaccines/appointments and school evaluation; admit only if asked gently. If shown empathy, soften, share more, and ask to minimize tests/visits due to cost and missed work.

medications: Immune globulin (IVIG) 400 mg/kg IV every 4 weeks
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 mg/kg/day (TMP component) PO daily for prophylaxis
comorbidities: Recurrent sinopulmonary infections (otitis media, bacterial pneumonia)
Iron overload secondary to chronic transfusion history
Mild transaminitis likely related to iron overload/chelation
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection triggering fever and elevated inflammatory markers
History of multiple transfusions affecting iron studies (elevated ferritin and transferrin saturation)
Chronic inflammation from recurrent infections causing anemia of inflammation overlap
Poor oral intake during febrile episodes contributing to low BMI and fatigue
Chelation therapy adverse effects (transaminitis) complicating interpretation of liver enzyme elevation

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 52-year-old female accounting manager. You are cooperative and speak clearly, matter-of-factly, but worried and tired.

At the start, volunteer: “I can’t stop having watery diarrhea,” many times daily, wakes you at night, you feel very thirsty/dried out, and you’re not very hungry.

If asked, add: unintentional weight loss, weakness/shakiness, leg muscle cramps, occasional racing heart, lightheaded on standing, and no blood in stool.

Do not volunteer hidden symptoms unless specifically asked about urination/hydration or dehydration signs: peeing less despite drinking, dry mouth/cracked lips, occasional flushed/warm skin, low energy.

Be reluctant to share resistant symptoms unless asked directly about accidents/sexual health; then admit a few accidents and avoiding sex due to embarrassment and exhaustion.

Express misconceptions: you thought it was food poisoning/stomach bug, tried cutting dairy/gluten without help, OTC anti-diarrhea barely works.

If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it briefly and become more open. Ask about costs/out-of-network scans and ongoing copays for long-term meds/injections.
